description: Manual ancestry links from oral tradition, tribal genealogy, and local knowledge. Captures relationships that Wikipedia can't provide.

relationshipTypes.descent: Blood lineage — this group descends from that group
relationshipTypes.confederation: Political union — this group is a member of that confederation (not blood descent)
relationshipTypes.pre_confederation_origin: Where a section originated BEFORE joining a confederation
relationshipTypes.branch: Split off from — same blood, formed separate entity

confidenceLevels.confirmed: Universally accepted, documented in academic sources
confidenceLevels.oral_tradition: Widely held in the region, passed down through families/tribal lore
confidenceLevels.claimed: Some genealogists assert this, disputed by others
confidenceLevels.legendary: Folk/mythological origin, part of cultural identity

links.note: Al Bu Falah is a section within the Bani Yas confederation — this is political membership, not necessarily blood descent
Al Bu Falasah is a section within Bani Yas. Split politically from Abu Dhabi to Dubai in 1833

Al Bu Falah section may trace deeper roots to the Dawasir tribal confederation of Najd before joining/forming Bani Yas. Not all Bani Yas sections share this origin — Bani Yas is a confederation of diverse groups
Al Nahyan are the ruling house of the Al Bu Falah section, ruling Abu Dhabi since 1793
Al Maktoum are the ruling house of Al Bu Falasah, ruling Dubai since 1833 split from Abu Dhabi
Al Bu Falasah branched from Al Bu Falah in 1833 under Maktoum bin Butti, migrating to Dubai

nonTribalOrigins.note: Persian/Iranian merchants who settled in Dubai Creek (Al Hor) area in early 19th century
From the town of Bastak in southern Iran, settled in Dubai and Bahrain
Hadrami origin from Wadi Doan, migrated to Saudi Arabia

formationTheories.note: Some accounts hold that Bani Yas formed as a defensive alliance against the Manasir tribe in the Abu Dhabi hinterland. The Bani Yas-Manasir rivalry is well-documented historically.
Traditional genealogists trace Bani Yas to a common ancestor (Yas bin Amer), making it a genuine kinship group. Other sections then joined through marriage and alliance over centuries.

formationTheories.theory: Hybrid — core kinship + later political additions
formationTheories.note: Most likely reality: a core group with shared ancestry that grew into a confederation as other groups joined for protection, trade, and territorial control. Over generations, intermarriage blurred the line between 'original' and 'allied' sections.

confederations.note: Bani Yas is a confederation with 30+ sections. Whether it started as a blood kinship group, a political alliance (possibly against the Manasir), or both is debated. The truth is probably messy — some sections share deep ancestry, others joined politically, and centuries of intermarriage make the distinction almost meaningless today.
Al Dawasir is itself a confederation of tribes from Wadi al-Dawasir in Najd. Some sections may have migrated to the Gulf coast and joined other confederations like Bani Yas.
